action.hypothesis: The first real `factorized_topk` proxy run produced a clean signal instead of a crash, so the family has earned one stabilization pass before retirement. The most informative single-variable follow-up is to increase dictionary width at fixed K=128 and fixed optimization settings, testing whether the low-rank pre-mixing path was quality-limited by latent capacity rather than by the factorized design itself.
action.summary: Run a `factorized_topk` proxy stabilization pass with `EXPANSION_FACTOR=32` at K=128 under the same signum baseline to test whether more latent capacity materially improves its proxy FVU.
action.self_review: This keeps the round to one coherent variable, preserves the now-working family, and answers the next obvious question after a clean but weak prototype result: was the architecture bottlenecked by capacity. The main risk is spending one more round on a family that is still well behind gated, but a width check is the cheapest way to determine whether `factorized_topk` has headroom or should be archived.
notes_to_memory: `factorized_topk` now has one valid proxy measurement: FVU 0.3275916539132595 at K=128, which is weak versus gated but good enough to justify one stabilization check instead of immediate retirement.
The next discriminating question is whether the family is fundamentally weak or merely under-capacity at the current width.
This round keeps architecture, K, optimizer, LR, and AUXK fixed and changes only expansion factor to probe latent-capacity sensitivity inside the incubating family.
next_hypotheses: If `factorized_topk` improves materially with higher expansion, keep the family alive and continue stabilizing its width/throughput tradeoff.
If higher expansion barely helps, archive `factorized_topk` and redirect architecture exploration to a different family.
If quality improves but runtime regresses sharply, follow with a targeted performance-focused round instead of discarding the family.
